reasoning: This is a simple data package that downloads two pre-built .dat files (geoip and geosite) from the official GitHub releases of the upstream maintainer (Loyalsoldier), pins SHA-256 checksums, and the package() function merely installs them into /usr/share/v2ray and /usr/share/xray. There is no pkgver() function, no top-level code execution, no build/prepare/check functions, and no .install hook. The .dat files are passive binary data with no executable content. Both models independently reached the same conclusion with accurate reasoning.
coverage_issues: Both models reviewed only PKGBUILD and one of the two .dat files (geosite). The geoip .dat file was not explicitly reviewed, though it is the same class of passive binary data and verified by pinned checksum, so the gap is low-risk.
Neither model explicitly confirmed the absence of a .install hook, though the package structure strongly implies none exists.
learnings: For data-only packages with pinned checksums and no build logic, two-file review is adequate; the 'shallow' trigger is a false alarm here since the package legitimately contains few security-relevant files.
Models correctly identified that pinned checksums on GitHub release artifacts mitigate supply-chain risk and that package() does not execute under makepkg's source-fetch gate.

The audit tool could special-case 'data dat/db file' packages to suppress shallow-review escalation when (a) PKGBUILD has no build/prepare/check/pkgver functions, (b) all sources are checksum-pinned, and (c) no .install hook is present.

Both models reasoned accurately and concisely; no prompt quality issues observed. Reviewing the second identical .dat (geoip) would have added marginal coverage with negligible additional risk insight.
